content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 13,
1994
Kevin Barnes
Refuse Superintendent
City of San Bernardino
300 North “D” Street
San Bernardino,
CA
92418
Re:
Your Request for Assistance
Our File No.
1-93-451
Dear Mr.
Barnes:
This is in response to your request for advice under the
conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reforiu Act.
1
!
Since you are only seeking general assistance and not inquiring
about a specific governmental decision,
we are treating your
letter as a request for informal assistance.
2
! The facts in this
letter restate information provided in your November 18,
1993
letter,
as well as information provided in our December 20,
1993
telephone conversation.
This letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oclesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
In addition,
issues may
arise under Section 1090 of the Government Code.
3
!
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory refer
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Com
mission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000 et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3) .)
3!
The Commission has no jurisdiction to interpret or enforce
Section 1090.
The Commission’s jurisdiction is limited to the
Political Reform Act.
Questions concerning Section 1090 should be
addressed to the Attorney General’s office or your County Counsel.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
1—93—451
Page 2
QUESTIONS
1.
Do you have an economic interest in a refuse collection
truck manufacturer who has agreed to build collection trucks in
accordance with the specifications of your patented design and
sell the trucks exclusively to you?
2.
May you sell your trucks to garbage haulers who are
located outside the city but may be doing business with the City
of San Bernardino?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Under the provisions of the Act, you have no economic
interest in the refuse collection truck manufacturer.
Therefore,
you would not be prohibited from participating in decisions which
may affect that manufacturer.
However, if the manufacturer should
become a source of income or gifts to you, you should contact this
office for further advice.
2.
The Act would not prohibit you from selling your trucks
to any garbage hauler.
However,
any garbage hauler who purchases
a truck for $250 or more will be a source of income to you.
You
may not participate in any decision which will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any source of income.
FACTS
You are the Superintendent of Sanitation for the City of San
Bernardino (the “city”).
In that capacity, you are responsible
for specifying, evaluating,
and recommending refuse equipment and
repairs for approval to the department director and to the city
council.
You designed and patented a refuse collection truck.
A
refuse equipment manufacturer,
Amrep, has agreed to manufacture
your truck under your specifications and sell it exclusively to
your business, Multi-Mode, at cost plus a profit.
Your solely-
owned business, Multi-Mode, will sell the trucks to garbage
haulers throughout the country.
iurep has,
in the past few years, provided refuse equipment,
parts and repairs to the city.
In addition, •the garbage haulers
purchasing your trucks while located outside the jurisdiction of
the city may do business with the city of San Bernardino.
You wish to receive advice regarding how your interaction with
iurep, as well as the sale to garbage haulers,
may affect your
position with the city.


Advice File No.
1-93-451
Page 3
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official’s immediate.
family, or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income
1 other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
V


Advice File No.
1—93—451
Page 4
“Income” as used above means a payment received,
including
but not limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend,
interest,
rent, proceeds from any sale, gift or loan.
Income of an
individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any
business entity in which the individual owns a 10 percent interest
or greater.
Income does not include income received from any source
outside the jurisdiction 4 not doing business within the
jurisdiction, not planning to do business within the jurisdiction,
or not having done business within the jurisdictionS within the
past two years.
(Section 82030.)
Under the agreement you propose, you will be paying Amrep its
cost plus profit for each truck it manufactures under your patent.
You have not provided any facts to indicate that rep will be
providing you with any income or with any investment interest in
its company.
If Ainrep manufactures your truck at a discount, or
at less than fair market value,
Amrep could be a source of income
or a source of a gift.
You have not indicated that this is the
case.
It does not appear that your agreement with Ainrep is an
economic interest under Section 87103.
However,
you have established a solely—owned business entity,
Multi—Mode, whidh will sell refuse collection trucks to garbage
haulers throughout the country.
Thus,
you have an investment
interest in Multi—Mode in accordance with Section 87103 (a).
Some
of the haulers, while located outside the city of San Bernardino,
do business within the city.
Any client of Multi-Mode which does
business within the city,
or is planning to do business in the
city, is source of income to you under Section 87103(c).
Accordingly,
you may not make, participate in making,
4
! or
use your official position to attempt to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
5
!
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on Multi—Mode, or on any source of income.
A public official participates in a governmental decision
when, acting within the authority of his or her position, the
public official negotiates, without significant substantive
review, with a governmental entity or private person regarding the
decision; or advises or makes recommendations to the decision—
maker.
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Advice File No.
1—93-451
Page 5
Material Financial Effect
The standard for determining materiality differs depending on
whether your economic interest is directly or indirectly affected
by the deqision.
The application of the direct versus indirect
standard is dependent on the specific facts of the decision.
You
do not, at this time, have a specific decision pending before you.
We discussed the application of the materiality standards at
length in our July 2,
1993 advice letter to you.
These rules
should be applied under these facts as well
(Barnes Advice Letter
No.
1—93—187.)
If,
in the future,
you have a specific decision
pending which may affect Multi-Mode or affect clients to Multi-
Mode,
and need assistance applying the appropriate materiality
standard, please contact this office.
Should you need any further assistance, please feel free to
contact the Commission’s Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
nse
B
:
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SC/JET!jt
